text.title: Screen-Reader Scripts — Core Rules
text.summary: Five testable rules every screen-reader test script must satisfy. Skipping any one produces audits that miss real-world failures or generate false negatives.

rule.statement: Each script provides steps for VoiceOver (macOS + iOS), NVDA (Windows), and TalkBack (Android). Platform-specific differences (gestures, modifier keys, navigation modes) are explicit; "test with a screen reader" without platform specifics is forbidden.

rationale.source: WebAIM Screen-Reader Survey; per-platform behaviour studies

rule.rationale: VoiceOver, NVDA, JAWS, and TalkBack interpret ARIA differently. A modal that passes VoiceOver may fail NVDA's focus trap; an autocomplete may announce correctly on desktop but not on mobile. Platform parity in the script catches the divergence.

rule.statement: Each step lists the EXPECTED utterance as a PATTERN, not an exact string. The pattern names required elements (role, accessible name, state) in order; surface phrasing differences are acceptable.

rationale.source: screen-reader localisation practice; cross-version utterance differences

rule.rationale: Exact-string matching fails across screen-reader versions, language packs, and verbosity settings. A pattern like `<name> , button , expanded` accommodates VoiceOver's "expanded" and NVDA's "expanded" and TalkBack's "expanded" without falsely failing on phrasing.

rule.statement: Each step in a script is mapped to one or more WCAG 2.2 success criteria (e.g. 1.3.1 Info and Relationships, 4.1.2 Name Role Value). Steps without WCAG mapping are not allowed.
rule.rationale: An audit finding without an SC traceback is unactionable for engineering and unconvincing in a conformance report. The mapping converts the failure into a fix request with legal weight.

rule.statement: Scripts have explicit fail-stop steps — focus trap broken, keyboard unreachable, no accessible name — that halt the script and mark the component non-compliant. Skipping over the failure to "test the rest" is forbidden.
rule.rationale: Continuing past a showstopper inflates the perceived complexity of fixes and hides the actual blocker. Fail-stop turns the audit output into a prioritised fix list: top showstoppers first.

rule.statement: Audit results MUST record the exact screen-reader versions, OS versions, browser versions, and target build version used. Drift between audits is forbidden without a re-test.

rationale.source: reproducibility in QA; SR-version-related regression literature

rule.rationale: A pass on VoiceOver 17.4 may be a fail on 17.5. Without pinning, audits cannot be compared across releases. Pinning makes regression tracking possible and protects the auditor from "we fixed that already, why are you finding it again?".

list.item: form: label binding, required, error association, group fieldset, submission feedback (SCs 1.3.1, 3.3.1, 3.3.2, 3.3.3, 4.1.2)
modal: focus trap, ESC close, return focus, aria-modal, accessible name (SCs 2.1.2, 2.4.3, 4.1.2)
table: header association, sort state, scope, caption (SCs 1.3.1, 1.3.2, 4.1.2)
navigation: landmark, current page, expanded state, menu pattern (SCs 1.3.1, 2.4.1, 4.1.2)
autocomplete (combobox): APG 1.2 pattern, listbox role, value-changed announce (SCs 1.3.1, 4.1.2, 4.1.3)
video player: caption toggle, control labels, transcript link, autoplay (SCs 1.2.2, 1.2.3, 2.2.2, 4.1.2)
toast / live region: aria-live polite vs assertive, role status / alert, dismiss (SCs 4.1.3)

example.title: Modal script excerpt (VoiceOver step)
example.code: Step 4 (VoiceOver / macOS / Safari): Pre: focus on the trigger button. Action: VO + space. Expected utterance pattern: "<modal accessible name> , dialog" Then VO will move focus to first focusable element inside. WCAG: 4.1.2 Name Role Value; 2.4.3 Focus Order. Fail-stop: if VO does not announce dialog role OR if focus stays outside.
example.note: Illustrates r1 (platform-specific), r2 (pattern), r3 (WCAG mapping), r4 (fail-stop named).

example.title: Abstract instruction without script
example.code: "Verify modal is accessible with screen readers."
example.note: r1, r2, r3, r4 all violated. The auditor will invent their own steps; results will not be comparable across audits.
